epigraph.text: His dominion is an everlasting dominion, and his kingdom endures from generation to generation.
epigraph.quote: A power series is a polynomial of infinite degree—if it converges.

content.text:
We begin with the general theory of power series: convergence, the 
radius of convergence, and term-by-term differentiation.

content.title: Power Series
content.content:
A **power series** centered at $z_0$ is an 
expression of the form
\[
 \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n (z - z_0)^n = a_0 + a_1(z - z_0) + a_2(z - z_0)^2 + \cdots
\]
where the **coefficients** $a_n \in \C$.

content.text:
For which values of $z$ does this series converge? The answer is 
beautifully geometric.

content.title: Radius of Convergence
content.content:
For any power series $\sum a_n(z - z_0)^n$, there exists a number 
$R \in [0, \infty]$ (the **radius of convergence**) 
such that:

1. The series converges absolutely for $|z - z_0| < R$.

2. The series diverges for $|z - z_0| > R$.

3. On $|z - z_0| = R$, behavior varies.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
The set $\{z : |z - z_0| < R\}$ is the **disk of convergence**. 
Unlike real analysis where intervals suffice, in $\C$ convergence 
regions are always disks (possibly of infinite radius).

content.title: Formulas for the Radius
content.content:
The radius of convergence is given by:
\[
 R = \frac{1}{\limsup_{n \to \infty} |a_n|^{1/n}}
\]
(Cauchy-Hadamard formula), or equivalently when the limit exists:
\[
 R = \lim_{n \to \infty} \left|\frac{a_n}{a_{n+1}}\right|.
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.problem: Find the radius of convergence of $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{z^n}{n!}$.
content.solution:
Using the ratio test: $\left|\frac{a_{n+1}}{a_n}\right| = \frac{n!}{(n+1)!} = \frac{1}{n+1} \to 0$.

Thus $R = 1/0 = \infty$. The series converges for all $z \in \C$—this 
is $e^z$.

content.title: Term-by-Term Operations
content.content:
Within the disk of convergence:

1. $f(z) = \sum a_n(z-z_0)^n$ is analytic.

2. $f'(z) = \sum n a_n(z-z_0)^{n-1}$ (same radius of convergence).

3. $\int f(z)\,dz = \sum \frac{a_n}{n+1}(z-z_0)^{n+1}$ (same radius).
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
Power series are "as nice as polynomials"—we can differentiate and 
integrate them term by term.

marginNotes.text:
All streams run to the sea, but the 
sea is not full; to the place where the streams flow, there they flow again.

marginNotes.author: Karl Weierstrass
marginNotes.text:
Power series are the natural language of 
analysis.
